student_name: João Guilherme da Costa e Silva
hub_subtitle: Business English técnico para reuniões com fornecedores internacionais, normas e entrevistas

lesson.menu_title: The Interview Room -- Performing Under Pressure
lesson.menu_desc: A entrevista em Bruxelas travou em marco. Hoje a gente faz a engenharia reversa dela: o metodo STAR, o used to / be used to / get used to que narra a sua evolucao profissional, e a versao de voce que sai da sala no controle -- 42 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 10 -- The Interview Room
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- João Guilherme | Lesson 10 -- The Interview Room

listenings.text: Hello João, this is Claire Mercier from Rail Systems International. Thank you for your application for the Senior Signaling Engineer role. I have read your profile, and I would like to invite you to the next stage, so let me tell you what to expect. It will be a panel interview, three of us, about forty-five minutes, and it is competency-based. That means we will not ask you to list what you know. We will ask you to tell us about times when you actually did something: a supplier who let you down, a test that failed, a decision you had to make with incomplete information. The clearest way to answer those is the STAR method, and I would recommend you prepare two or three real examples before we speak. One more thing, and please read between the lines here. We are less interested in whether you have worked on our exact product, and more interested in whether your skills transfer and whether you are the right culture fit for the team. So do not sell yourself short because your background is electrification rather than our signaling platform. Finally, two practical points I need to raise early: the role is based in Zurich with relocation support, and I will need to understand your notice period. We can cover both at the end. I look forward to it. Speak soon.

Right, before your panel on Thursday, three things, because good engineers interview badly more often than anybody else, and it is nearly always the same three mistakes. First, you sell yourself short. You say I only did this, or I just helped with that, and the word only is doing terrible work in that sentence. You led the interface between three suppliers on a live commissioning. Say that. Nobody in the room will respect you more for shrinking it. Second, you answer the question you wish they had asked, not the one they asked. When they say tell me about a time you managed a conflict, they do not want your philosophy of conflict. They want one situation, one task, one action, one result. Cut to the chase, name the story in the first sentence, and let the STAR structure carry the rest. Third, and this is the one that separates a good candidate from the one who gets the offer: think on your feet, but do not rush. When they ask something you did not prepare, it is fine to say, that is a good question, let me think for a second. That pause makes you come across as calm and senior, not slow. Silence is not your enemy in an interview. Panic is. You have the track record. On Thursday, all you have to do is describe it like it belongs to you.

patterns.title: Three ways to say "used to" -- and the modals that position you
cards: I used to work on electrification

used to + base verb. A state or habit that was true before and is finished. It is how you narrate where you came FROM. "I used to work on electrification projects before I specialized in signaling." Negative and question drop the -d: "I didn't use to...", "Did you use to...?"
I am used to managing suppliers

be used to + noun / -ing. You are accustomed to it; it is normal for you now. NOT the base verb -- the -ing. "I'm used to managing suppliers in three time zones." "I'm not used to being interviewed in English, but I've prepared."
I got used to reading CENELEC in English
get used to + noun / -ing. The change from strange to normal, over time. "It took a while, but I got used to reading CENELEC standards in English." "You will get used to the pace."
I would rather / had better / may as well

would rather + base verb = preference ("I would rather be judged on the result"). had better + base verb = strong advice, almost a warning ("I had better give you a concrete example"). may as well + base verb = there is no better option, so let's ("We may as well start with the hardest project").

patterns.text: The trap is the verb form. used to + BASE VERB (past habit). be / get used to + -ING (accustomed). "I used to manage" = I did, and I stopped. "I am used to managing" = it is normal for me. Same three words, opposite meanings -- and an interviewer hears the difference.

reading.rtitle: Senior Signaling Engineer (ETCS / CBTC) -- International Projects -- job posting, extract
paras: Rail Systems International is expanding its signaling delivery teams across Europe and the Middle East, and we are looking for a Senior Signaling Engineer who is used to working with international suppliers and multi-disciplinary teams. The successful candidate will have owned the interface between design, installation and commissioning on at least one main-line or metro project, and will be comfortable reading CENELEC and IEC standards in English. You will not need to be told that a comma in a technical clause can change the scope of the work.

We value transferable skills over a specific product background. An engineer who used to work on electrification and got used to signaling is exactly the profile we want, because we hire for range, not for a single platform. What matters most to us is culture fit: we work in the open, we escalate early, and we would rather hear about a problem on the first day than discover it as a surprise at handover. If you are the kind of engineer who names the risk before anyone asks, you will do well here.

The role is based in Zurich, with regular travel to project sites; relocation support is provided for the successful candidate and their family. A notice period of up to three months is not an obstacle for the right person, so please do not let your current commitments stop you from applying. Applications are reviewed on a rolling basis, and shortlisted candidates will be invited to a competency-based panel interview conducted in English.
reading.source: Rail Systems International -- Careers page, role RSI-SIG-214 (fictional, written for João's package)

reading.text: Notice the collocations of the job market: value transferable skills, be based in, provide relocation support, serve a notice period, review applications on a rolling basis, shortlist a candidate, invite to a panel interview, hire for range.

gist.prompt: What is this posting really telling you to do?
choices: Only apply if you have already worked on their exact signaling platform, because product experience is what they screen for.
Do not rule yourself out over background or notice period: they hire for transferable skills and culture fit, and they want engineers who name risk early.
The role is administrative and office-based, so travel and site experience are not important.
Relocation is impossible unless you already live in Switzerland.

items: The company will only consider candidates who have already worked on its own signaling platform.
f

The opposite. "We value transferable skills over a specific product background... we hire for range, not for a single platform." An electrification engineer who moved into signaling is named as exactly the profile they want.
Being comfortable reading CENELEC and IEC standards in English is part of what they expect.
t
"...will be comfortable reading CENELEC and IEC standards in English. You will not need to be told that a comma in a technical clause can change the scope." That is aula 8, quoted back at you.
Culture fit for them means keeping problems quiet until you have a solution.
f

The reverse. "We work in the open, we escalate early, and we would rather hear about a problem on the first day than discover it as a surprise at handover." They reward the engineer who names the risk before anyone asks.
A three-month notice period would automatically disqualify you.
f
"A notice period of up to three months is not an obstacle for the right person." They tell you not to let current commitments stop you from applying.
Shortlisted candidates face a competency-based panel interview in English.
t
"...shortlisted candidates will be invited to a competency-based panel interview conducted in English." Competency-based means behavioral questions -- exactly what STAR is built for.

items.situation: "Tell me about yourself." You have ninety seconds. Do not narrate your whole CV.
tips: I'm a signaling engineer at Motiva. I used to work on electrification, and over the last three years I got used to owning the interface between design, installation and commissioning.
The part I'm proudest of is the international side: I'm used to running technical calls with German, French and Chinese suppliers, in English, under schedule pressure.

items.situation: "Why are you leaving Motiva?" Stay positive; do not criticize your employer.
tips: I've learned a great deal at Motiva, and I would rather leave for something than away from something.
I'm ready for a role with more international scope and a bigger interface to own, and that is exactly what this position offers.

items.situation: "Tell me about a time you managed a conflict with an international supplier." Use STAR -- name the story first.
tips: In 2024, a German supplier argued that six failed units were an installation problem, not a design one. My task was to keep the design question open without stopping the line.
What I did was accept the six in writing and put the shared grounding design on record, and as a result they re-scanned the units that passed and re-verified the configuration at their cost.

items.situation: "What is your biggest weakness in an international environment?" Be honest, then show the fix.
tips: I used to sell myself short in English -- I would say I only helped, when I had actually led. I'm getting used to describing my work as it really was.
So now I prepare concrete examples in advance, and I would rather give you one real result than a list of adjectives.

items.situation: They ask something you did not prepare. Buy yourself a second without looking lost.
tips: That is a good question -- let me think for a second.
I want to give you a real example rather than a general answer, so give me a moment to pick the right one.

items.situation: "Where do you see yourself in five years?" Ambition, but grounded in the role.
tips: I'd like to be leading signaling delivery on a full project, not just an interface -- and I'd rather build that here than start again somewhere else.
In the short term, I may as well be honest: my first year is about getting used to your standards and earning that scope.

bank.label: Narrating where you came from (used to + base verb)
items: I used to work on electrification projects before I specialized in signaling.
I used to sell myself short in English, and I have deliberately trained myself out of it.
Early on I used to answer the question I wished they had asked; now I answer the one they asked.

bank.label: Saying what is normal for you now (be used to + -ing)
items: I'm used to managing suppliers across three time zones on a live commissioning.
I'm used to reading CENELEC and IEC standards in English and working directly from them.
I'm not used to being interviewed in English, but I have prepared real examples so I can be precise.

bank.label: Describing the process of adapting (get used to + -ing)
items: It took time, but I got used to running technical calls at native speed.
When I was deployed to São Francisco in 2023, I got used to making decisions with incomplete information.
I expect to get used to your standards quickly, because that is exactly what I did at Motiva.

bank.label: Positioning yourself (would rather / had better / may as well)
items: I would rather give you one concrete result than a list of skills.
I had better be specific here, so let me give you the numbers.
We may as well start with the hardest project I have worked on.
